title: Wound dressings and systems with low-flow therapeutic gas sources for topical wound therapy and related methods
abstract: This disclosure includes wound dressings and systems with low-flow, high- concentration therapeutic gas sources for topical wound therapy and related methods. Some dressings, which are configured to be coupled to tissue to facilitate delivery of therapeutic gas to the tissue, comprise a manifold that defines a plurality of gas passageways, the manifold configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas to the tissue; a sorbent material configured to be disposed above or below the manifold and to capture exudate; and a gas- occlusive layer configured to be disposed over the manifold and the sorbent material and coupled to the tissue such that an interior volume containing the manifold and the sorbent material is defined between the gas-occlusive layer and the tissue and the gas-occlusive layer limits escape of therapeutic gas from the interior volume.
claims:
Claims 1. A dressing configured to be coupled to tissue to facilitate delivery of therapeutic gas to the tissue, the dressing comprising: a manifold that defines a plurality of gas passageways, the manifold configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas to the tissue; an absorbent layer including a foam or a non-woven textile, the absorbent layer configured to be disposed below the manifold and to draw exudate away from the tissue; a patient-interface layer configured to be disposed below the absorbent layer and in contact with the tissue, the patient-interface layer defining a plurality of openings configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas and exudate through the patient-interface layer; and a gas-occlusive layer configured to be disposed over the manifold and coupled to the tissue such that an interior volume containing the manifold is defined between the gas-occlusive layer and the tissue and the gas-occlusive layer limits escape of therapeutic gas from the interior volume. 2. The dressing of claim 1, wherein the absorbent layer has a plurality of perforations and/or openings configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas to the tissue. 3. The dressing of claim 1 or 2, wherein the absorbent layer comprises a hydrophilic material, optionally, a superab sorbent polymer. 4. The dressing of any of claims 1-3, comprising a sorbent material configured to be disposed below the gas-occlusive layer and to capture exudate. 5. The dressing of claim 4, comprising a sorbent layer that includes the sorbent material. 6. The dressing of claim 5, wherein: the sorbent layer has a plurality of perforations; the sorbent layer has one or more openings; and/or the sorbent layer has a textured surface comprising a plurality of grooves. 7. The dressing of claim 5 or 6, wherein a planform area of the sorbent layer is at least 5 percent smaller than a planform area of the manifold. 8. The dressing of any of claims 5-7, wherein the sorbent layer comprises an absorbent material. 9. The dressing of claim 8, wherein the absorbent material comprises a foam, a non-woven textile, or a superab sorbent polymer. 10. The dressing of any of claims 5-7, wherein the sorbent layer comprises an adsorbent material. 11. The dressing of claim 10, wherein the adsorbent material comprises a carbon filter. 12. The dressing of any of claims 1-11, wherein the patient-interface layer comprises a polymer, optionally, silicone, polyethylene, ethylene vinyl acetate, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 13. The dressing of any of claims 1-12, wherein the patient- interface layer includes an adhesive configured to couple the patient-interface layer to the tissue. 14. A dressing configured to be coupled to tissue to facilitate delivery of therapeutic gas to the tissue, the dressing comprising: a manifold that defines a plurality of gas passageways, the manifold configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas to the tissue; a liquid control layer having a plurality of perforations, the liquid control layer configured to be disposed below the manifold to restrict communication of exudate toward the tissue; a gas-occlusive layer configured to be disposed over the manifold and coupled to the tissue such that an interior volume containing the manifold is defined between the gas-occlusive layer and the tissue and the gas-occlusive layer limits escape of therapeutic gas from the interior volume. 15. The dressing of claim 14, comprising a sorbent material configured to be disposed between the manifold and the liquid control layer and to capture exudate. 16. A dressing configured to be coupled to tissue to facilitate delivery of therapeutic gas to the tissue, the dressing comprising: a manifold that defines a plurality of gas passageways, the manifold configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas to the tissue; 

 a sorbent material configured to be disposed above or below the manifold and to capture exudate; and a liquid control layer having a plurality of perforations, the liquid control layer configured to be disposed below the sorbent material to restrict communication of exudate toward the tissue; and a gas-occlusive layer configured to be disposed over the manifold and the sorbent material and coupled to the tissue such that an interior volume containing the manifold and the sorbent material is defined between the gas-occlusive layer and the tissue and the gas-occlusive layer limits escape of therapeutic gas from the interior volume. 17. The dressing of claim 15 or 16, comprising a sorbent layer that includes the sorbent material. 18. The dressing of claim 17, wherein: the sorbent layer has a plurality of perforations; the sorbent layer has one or more openings; and/or the sorbent layer has a textured surface comprising a plurality of grooves. 19. The dressing of claim 17 or 18, wherein a planform area of the sorbent layer is at least 5 percent smaller than a planform area of the manifold. 20. The dressing of any of claims 17-19, wherein the sorbent layer comprises an absorbent material. 21. The dressing of claim 20, wherein the absorbent material comprises a foam, a non- woven textile, or a superabsorbent polymer. 22. The dressing of any of claims 17-19, wherein the sorbent layer comprises an adsorbent material. 23. The dressing of claim 22, wherein the adsorbent material comprises a carbon filter. 24. The dressing of any of claims 14-23, wherein the liquid control layer comprises a foam or a non- woven textile. 25. The dressing of any of claims 14-24, wherein the liquid control layer comprises a hydrophilic material, optionally, a superabsorbent material. 26. The dressing of any of claims 14-21, wherein the liquid control layer comprises a film. 27. The dressing of any of claims 14-26, comprising a patient-interface layer configured to be disposed in contact with the tissue, the patient interface layer defining a plurality of openings configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas and exudate through the patient-interface layer. 28. The dressing of claim 27, wherein the patient-interface layer comprises a polymer, optionally, silicone, polyethylene, ethylene vinyl acetate, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 29. The dressing of claim 27 or 28, wherein the patient-interface layer includes an adhesive configured to couple the patient-interface layer to the tissue. 30. The dressing of any of claims 1-29, wherein the manifold comprises polyethylene, a polyolefin, a polyether, polyurethane, a co-polyester, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 31. The dressing of any of claims 1-30, wherein the manifold comprises a foam or a non- woven textile. 32. A dressing configured to be coupled to tissue to facilitate delivery of therapeutic gas to the tissue, the dressing comprising: a first manifold layer and a second manifold layer, each defining a plurality of gas passageways, the first and second manifold layers configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas to the tissue; a sorbent material configured to be disposed between the first and second manifold layers and to capture exudate; and a gas-occlusive layer configured to be disposed over the manifold and coupled to the tissue such that an interior volume containing the first and second manifold layers is defined between the gas-occlusive layer and the tissue and the gas- occlusive layer limits escape of therapeutic gas from the interior volume. 33. The dressing of claim 32, comprising a sorbent layer that includes the sorbent material. 34. The dressing of claim 33, wherein: the sorbent layer has a plurality of perforations; the sorbent layer has one or more openings; and/or the sorbent layer has a textured surface comprising a plurality of grooves. 35. The dressing of claim 33 or 34, wherein a planform area of the sorbent layer is at least 5 percent smaller than each of a planform area of the first manifold layer and a planform area of the second manifold layer. 36. The dressing of any of claims 32-35, wherein the sorbent material comprises an absorbent material. 37. The dressing of claim 36, wherein the absorbent material comprises a foam, a non- woven textile, or a superabsorbent polymer. 38. The dressing of any of claims 32-35, wherein the sorbent material comprises an adsorbent material. 39. The dressing of claim 38, wherein the adsorbent material comprises a carbon filter. 40. The dressing of any of claims 32-39, comprising a liquid control layer having a plurality of perforations, the liquid control layer configured to be disposed below the sorbent material to restrict communication of exudate toward the tissue. 41. The dressing of claim 40, wherein the liquid control layer comprises a foam or a non- woven textile. 42. The dressing of claim 40 or 41 , wherein the liquid control layer comprises a hydrophilic material, optionally, a superabsorbent material. 43. The dressing of any of claim 40, wherein the liquid control layer comprises a film. 44. The dressing of any of claims 32-43, comprising a patient-interface layer configured to be disposed in contact with the tissue, the patient interface layer defining a plurality of openings configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas and exudate through the patient-interface layer. 45. The dressing of claim 44, wherein the patient-interface layer comprises a polymer, optionally, silicone, polyethylene, ethylene vinyl acetate, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 46. The dressing of claim 44 or 45, wherein the patient interface layer includes an adhesive configured to couple the patient-interface layer to the tissue. 47. The dressing of any of claims 32-46, wherein at least one of the first and second manifold layers comprises polyethylene, a polyolefin, a polyether, polyurethane, a co- polyester, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 48. The dressing of any of claims 32-47, wherein at least one of the first and second manifold layers comprises a foam or a non- woven textile. 49. A dressing configured to be coupled to tissue to facilitate delivery of therapeutic gas to the tissue, the dressing comprising: a manifold that defines a plurality of gas passageways, the manifold configured to allow communication of therapeutic gas to the tissue; and a gas-occlusive layer configured to be disposed over the manifold and coupled to the tissue such that an interior volume containing the manifold is defined between the gas-occlusive layer and the tissue and the gas-occlusive layer limits escape of therapeutic gas from the interior volume. 50. The dressing of any of claims 1-49, comprising: one or more ports coupled to or defined by the gas-occlusive layer; wherein the one or more ports are configured to permit communication of therapeutic gas through the gas-occlusive layer and into the interior volume. 51. The dressing of claim 50, comprising, for at least one of the one or more ports, a filter configured to filter fluid that flows through the port. 52. The dressing of claim 51 , wherein the filter comprises a layer of material that is bonded to an upper surface or a lower surface of the gas-occlusive layer. 53. The dressing of claim 51 or 52, wherein the filter comprises polytetrafluoroethylene, a polyester, a polyamide, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 54. The dressing of any of claims 1-53, comprising a valve coupled to the gas-occlusive layer and configured to relieve pressure within the interior volume when pressure within the interior volume exceeds a threshold pressure. 55. The dressing of claim 54, wherein the valve comprises a one-way valve configured to: permit communication of gas out of the interior volume through the valve; and prevent communication of gas into the interior volume through the valve. 56. The dressing of claim 54 or 55, wherein the valve comprises a thin film valve or a check valve. 57. The dressing of any of claims 1-56, comprising a sensor configured to detect a presence of therapeutic gas within the interior volume. 58. The dressing of claim 57, wherein the sensor is configured to detect a presence of oxygen within the interior volume. 59. The dressing of claim 58, wherein the sensor comprises a material configured to be disposed within the interior volume and to change color in response to a change in oxygen concentration within the interior volume. 60. The dressing of claim 59, wherein the material comprises a pressure- sensitive paint. 61. The dressing of claim 59, wherein the material comprises a redox indicator. 62. The dressing of claim 61, wherein the redox indicator comprises methylene blue, phenosafranine, indigo carmine, resazurin, N-phenylanthranilic acid, and/or neutral red. 63. The dressing of any of claims 57-62, wherein the material is disposed on a lower surface of the gas-occlusive layer. 64. The dressing of claim 58, wherein the sensor comprises: a layered silicate; a cationic surfactant; an organic colorant; and a reducing agent. 65. The dressing of any of claims 1-64, comprising a sensor configured to detect a pH of fluid within the interior volume. 66. The dressing of claim 65, wherein the sensor comprises a material configured to be disposed within the interior volume and to change color in response to a change in pH of fluid within the interior volume. 67. The dressing of claim 66, wherein the material is configured to absorb carbon dioxide. 68. The dressing of claim 66, wherein the material is configured to absorb ammonia. 69. The dressing of any of claims 66-68, wherein the material is disposed on a lower surface of the gas-occlusive layer. 70. The dressing of any of claims 1-69, wherein the gas-occlusive layer includes an adhesive configured to couple the gas-occlusive layer to the tissue. 71. The dressing of any of claims 1-70, wherein the gas-occlusive layer has a thickness that is between approximately 15 micrometers (Î¼Î¹Î·) and approximately 40 Î¼Î¹Î·. 72. The dressing of any of claims 1-71, wherein the gas-occlusive layer comprises polyurethane, polyethylene, polyvinyl acetate, polyvinyl chloride, polyvinylidene chloride, isobutylene, a halogenated isomer, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 73. A system comprising: the dressing of any of claims 1-72; an oxygen source; and a conduit configured to be coupled between the oxygen source and the dressing to permit communication of oxygen from the oxygen source into the interior volume of the dressing. 74. The system of claim 73, wherein the oxygen source comprises an electrolytic oxygen source. 75. The system of claim 73 or 74, wherein the oxygen source is configured to produce oxygen at a flow rate that is less than approximately 100 milliliters per hour (mL/hour), optionally, less than approximately 50 mL/hour. 76. The system of any of claims 73-75, wherein the conduit includes: an elongated core comprising a foam or a non- woven textile; and a sheath comprising a gas-occlusive film; wherein the sheath is disposed around and extends along at least a majority of a length of the core. 77. A system comprising: the dressing of any of claims 1-72; and a conduit configured to be coupled to the dressing to permit communication of therapeutic gas into the interior volume of the dressing, the conduit comprising: an elongated core comprising a foam or a non- woven textile; and a sheath comprising a gas-occlusive film; wherein the sheath is disposed around and extends along at least a majority of a length of the core. 78. The system of claim 76 or 77, wherein a thickness of the core is less than 10 percent of a width of the core. 79. The system of any of claims 76-78, wherein the sheath comprises polyurethane, polyethylene, polyvinyl acetate, polyvinyl chloride, polyvinylidene chloride, isobutylene, a halogenated isomer, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 80. The system of any of claims 76-79, wherein the core comprises polyethylene, a polyolefin, a polyether, polyurethane, a co-polyester, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 81. A method comprising: coupling the dressing of any of claims 1-72 to a patient's tissue; and introducing therapeutic gas into the interior volume of the dressing. 82. The method of claim 81, wherein introducing therapeutic gas into the interior volume of the dressing is performed at a flow rate that is less than approximately 100 mL/hour, optionally, less than approximately 50 mL/hour. 83. The method of claim 81 or 82, wherein introducing therapeutic gas into the interior volume of the dressing comprises introducing oxygen into the interior volume of the dressing. 84. The method of claim 83, wherein oxygen introduced into the interior volume of the dressing is produced via electrolysis. 85. The method of any of claims 81-84, wherein introducing therapeutic gas into the interior volume of the dressing is performed via a conduit including: an elongated core comprising a foam or a non- woven textile; and a sheath comprising a gas-occlusive film; wherein the sheath is disposed around and extends along at least a majority of a length of the core. 86. The method of claim 85, wherein a thickness of the core is less than 10 percent of a width of the core. 87. The method of claim 85 or 86, wherein the sheath comprises polyurethane, polyethylene, polyvinyl acetate, polyvinyl chloride, polyvinylidene chloride, isobutylene, a halogenated isomer, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 88. The method of any of claims 85-87 wherein the core comprises polyethylene, a polyolefin, a polyether, polyurethane, a co-polyester, a copolymer thereof, or a blend thereof. 89. The method of any of claims 81-88, comprising, prior to introducing therapeutic gas into the interior volume of the dressing, reducing pressure within the interior volume.
